ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE
COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
PASSED AT THE REGULAR SESSION OF THE GENERAL
ASSEMBLY AT FRANKFORT , KENTUCKY , BEING THE
REGULAR SESSION OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY FOR
THE COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY FOR THE YEAR
ONE THOUSAND NINE HUNDRED AND TWENTY (1920).
CHAPTER 1 .
AN ACT to promote safetyto the travelingpublicin this
Commonwealth and to provide a penalty for a violation
of same .
Be itenactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That it shallbe unlawful for any person,
or persons, firm, corporation or company to erect
on or near the publicroads and highways of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky , any sign board or
other contrivance for the purpose of advertisement
or for any other purpose, similarto or likethe
danger signalsused by the railroads, interurbans
and electricrailway companies at road crossings,
and all such signs heretoforeerected shall be
taken down within six months after this Act
becomes a law .
§ 2. That any person, persons, firm, corpora-
tion, or company who shallviolateSection 1 of
this Act shall be fined not less than five nor more
than one hundred dollars for each offense .
Approved February 19, 1920.

4 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
In Henderson county the Henderson Circuit
Court shallbe held at Henderson , Kentucky ,and
the terms of said court shallbegin on the first
Monday in the months of January and September
of each year and continue,if the businessrequires
it,forforty-eightjuridicaldays in each term. The
term beginning the firstMonday in May shall
continue for thirtyjuridicaldays ifthe business
requires it.
In Union County the Union CircuitCourt shall
be held at Morganfield, Kentucky ,and the terms
of said court shallbegin on the firstMonday in
the months of March and November and on the
second Monday in the month of June . The
terms of saidcourtbeginningon the firstMonday
in March and November shallcontinue for twenty-
fourjuridicaldays each term,and the term begin-
ning on the second Monday in the month of June
shallcontinueeighteenjuridicaldays ,ifthe busi-
ness requires it.
In Webster County the Webster CircuitCourt
shallbe held at Dixon , Kentucky , and the said
terms of said court shallbegin on the firstMonday
in the months of April,July and December , and
continue, if the businessrequiresit, for twenty-
four juridicaldays in each term .
2. All Acts and parts of Acts in conflictwith
this Act are hereby repealed. Inasmuch as the
time forholding court in one of the counties under
the presentlaw comes in the month of June ,and
in orderto have the courtheld under the provision
of thisAct , an emergency is declared to exist.
3. This Act shall take effect and be in force
from and after itsapproval by the Governor .
Approved February 27,1920 .

Be itenactedby theGeneralAssembly oftheCom-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That upon the concurrenceof three-fifths

tucky,the yeas and nays being taken thereon and

upon the per capitabasis."
§ 2. This amendment shallbe submitted to

rejectionat the time and in the manner provided
for under Section 256 of the Constitution of
Kentucky , and under Section 1459 of Edition of
Kentucky Statutescompiled and editedby John
D. Carroll and issued in 1915 .
Approved March 5,1920 .

and re-enacted, so that said section, when so

classification.title: Restrictions on Candidacy After Primary Election
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which candidates who have been defeated in primary elections are prohibited from running for the same office in the general election within the same year. It reflects mechanisms that could disproportionately affect certain groups, particularly in the context of Jim Crow laws.
classification.reasoning: The statute does not explicitly mention race, but it establishes rules that could be used to disenfranchise certain groups, particularly Black Kentuckians, in the context of Jim Crow laws. The prohibition on running for office after a primary defeat could be seen as a way to limit political participation among those already marginalized. Given the historical context and the potential implications of the law, it is classified as a Jim Crow law, but the lack of explicit racial language leads to a need for human review.

classification.title: Death Penalty for Rape
classification.summary: This statute establishes the death penalty for the crime of rape, with executions to be carried out in the county where the crime was committed. The differential treatment of crimes, particularly severe penalties for certain offenses, can be interpreted as a mechanism that disproportionately affects marginalized communities, including Black Kentuckians.
classification.reasoning: The statute specifies a severe penalty (death) for the crime of rape, which historically has been applied in a racially discriminatory manner, particularly against Black individuals. While it does not explicitly mention race, the context of the Jim Crow era and the enforcement of laws suggest that this statute could be used to disproportionately target Black Kentuckians. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law with implicit racial implications.
